curriculum.subject: Mathematics
curriculum.overallDescription: 6 units, 23 chapters, and 36 topics extracted through raw extraction, heading classification, normalization, validation, and statistics generation.
coreObjectives: Understand the concept of a rational number.
Represent rational numbers on the number line.
Understand the properties of rational numbers.
Explain the concept of density of rational numbers.
Compute decimal representation of rational numbers.
Understand the concept of irrational numbers.
Apply computational thinking to represent rational and irrational numbers through algorithms and visual models.
Understand the meaning of an algebraic expression.

units.description: Introduction to Polynomials, Sequences and Progressions, Exploring Algebraic Identities, Linear Equations in Two Variables, Algebra
topics: Graphical method for solving pairs of linear equations

units.description: Introduction to Euclid's Geometry, Lines and Angles, Triangles: Congruence Theorems, Quadrilaterals, Circles, Geometry
topics: Triangle Congruence Conditions (SAS, SSS, ASA, RHS, AAS)

units.description: Perimeter of shapes, Heron's formula, Brahmagupta's formula, Squaring a rectangle, Circumference and Arc length, Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere), Mensuration
Statistics, Introduction to Probability, Statistics and Probability

classes.subject: Mathematics
classes.part_or_section: Curriculum Structure and Question Paper Design

headings: Representation of rational numbers on the number line
Density of rational numbers and its proof
Finding rational numbers between any two rational numbers

subtopics: Graphical method for solving pairs of linear equations

headings: Distance between two points in the 2-D plane
Midpoint of the line-segment between two points
subtopics: Representing floor plans on a grid

headings: Introduction to Euclid's Geometry: Axioms and Postulates
topics: Triangle Congruence Conditions (SAS, SSS, ASA, RHS, AAS)
subtopics: Angle subtended by an arc at the centre and circumference

possible_chapters: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
subtopics: Special cases vs Generalisation in geometry
Area of sector and segment of circle
Tree diagrams and tables for probability

practicals.title: Lab Practical (Mathematics Laboratory)
practicals.source_text: Lab activities to be done from the prescribed books. Prescribed Books: Mathematics - Textbook for class IX, Guidelines for Mathematics Laboratory in Schools, class IX, Laboratory Manual - Mathematics, secondary stage.

activities.title: Square Root Spiral Construction
activities.source_text: Construct the square root spiral. Apply computational thinking to represent rational and irrational numbers through algorithms and visual models.

activities.title: Tower of Hanoi Puzzle
activities.source_text: Solve the Tower of Hanoi puzzle. Use computational thinking to identify patterns, write step-by-step rules.

activities.title: Geometric Models for Identities
activities.source_text: Visualise algebraic identities using geometric models. Visualising factorisation of quadratic expressions through algebra tiles.

activities.title: Floor Plan Representation
activities.source_text: Represent a floor plan on a grid using coordinates. Compute the distance between two points.

activities.title: Euclid's Definitions and Constructions
activities.source_text: Constructing a square with a given side as described in the Baudhayana's Sulbasutras. Justify simple constructions using axioms.

activities.title: Paper Manipulation Activities
activities.source_text: Engage in drawing, measurement and paper manipulation activities to discover geometric patterns involving triangles and 4-gons.

activities.title: Cultural Motifs Analysis
activities.source_text: Identify cultural motifs involving circles, for example, the Dharmachakra, Ashoka Chakra, Sudarshan Chakra.

activities.title: Squaring a Rectangle Proof
activities.source_text: Explain the classical problem of 'squaring' a given shape. Explain how ancient civilisations approximated the area of a circle.

activities.title: Data Analysis Projects
activities.source_text: Collect, organise, visualise and interpret data to answer a statistical investigative question. Apply computational thinking strategies to analyse real-life data.

activities.title: Random Experiment Simulation
activities.source_text: Use computational thinking strategies, such as pattern recognition and simulation, to model random experiments and estimate probabilities.

projects.title: Portfolio Assessment
projects.source_text: Internal Assessment: Portfolio (05 Marks). Continuous and holistic assessment through projects, activities, and investigations forms an integral part of the learning process.

projects.title: Mathematical Modelling Projects
projects.source_text: Models daily-life phenomena and uses representations (graphs, tables, equations) to draw conclusions. Models real-life situations in the form of equations.

marks_distribution: - Pen Paper Test and Multiple Assessment: 10 Marks
teacher_notes: Curriculum redesigned in alignment with NEP 2020 and NCF-SE 2023.
Prioritize deep conceptual understanding, logical reasoning, and competency-based education over rote learning.
Integrate Indian Knowledge System (e.g., Baudhayana's Sulbasutras, Brahmagupta, Aryabhata) with contemporary knowledge.
Focus on problem-solving, visualisation, mathematical modelling, communication, computational thinking, and data analytics.
Emphasis on real-life applications and experiential learning.
Continuous assessment through projects, activities, and investigations is integral; move beyond summative examinations only.
Prescribed Books: NCERT Textbook for Class IX, CBSE Guidelines for Mathematics Laboratory in Schools (Class IX), NCERT Laboratory Manual.

classified_blocks.source_text_excerpt: Unit covering rational and irrational numbers, density proofs, decimal representations, and square root spirals.
Basic concepts of rational numbers within the Number System unit.

classified_blocks.source_heading: Representation of rational numbers on the number line
classified_blocks.source_text_excerpt: Visualizing and plotting rational points on a linear scale.

classified_blocks.source_heading: Density of rational numbers and its proof
classified_blocks.source_text_excerpt: Mathematical proofs demonstrating the density property.

classified_blocks.source_heading: Finding rational numbers between any two rational numbers
classified_blocks.source_text_excerpt: Methods to identify intermediate rational values.
Converting fractions to terminating or recurring decimals.
Definition and characteristics of non-rational real numbers.
Logical proofs establishing the non-existence as fractions.
Geometric construction illustrating irrational lengths.
Unit covering polynomials, sequences, identities, and linear equations.
Fundamentals of algebraic expressions involving variables and coefficients.
Study of arithmetic and geometric progressions.
Derivation and application of standard algebraic formulas.
Solving and graphing equations with two unknowns.
Unit covering the Cartesian system, distance formula, and midpoint calculations.
Historical context of the development of coordinate systems.
Structure and axes of the two-dimensional plane.

classified_blocks.source_heading: Distance between two points in the 2-D plane
classified_blocks.source_text_excerpt: Formula and application for calculating Euclidean distance.

classified_blocks.source_heading: Midpoint of the line-segment between two points
classified_blocks.source_text_excerpt: Determining the center point of a segment connecting two coordinates.
Unit covering Euclid's axioms, lines, triangles, quadrilaterals, and circles.

classified_blocks.source_heading: Introduction to Euclid's Geometry: Axioms and Postulates
classified_blocks.source_text_excerpt: Foundational principles of classical geometry.
Properties of lines, rays, angles, and parallelism.
Conditions for triangle congruency (SAS, SSS, etc.).
Properties and theorems related to four-sided polygons.
Definitions of chords, arcs, and properties of cyclic quadrilaterals.
Unit covering area, perimeter, surface area, and volume calculations.
Formulas for calculating boundary lengths and enclosed areas of shapes.
Calculations for 3D shapes including cubes, cylinders, cones, pyramids, and spheres.
Unit covering data representation, central tendency measures, and probability concepts.
Visualizing data using stacked bar graphs and other charts.
Calculating mean, median, mode, and weighted averages.
Understanding the scale of probability and random events.
Comparing experimental results with calculated theoretical likelihoods.

teacher_notes: Curriculum redesigned in alignment with NEP 2020 and NCF-SE 2023.
Prioritize deep conceptual understanding, logical reasoning, and competency-based education over rote learning.
Integrate Indian Knowledge System (e.g., Baudhayana's Sulbasutras, Brahmagupta, Aryabhata) with contemporary knowledge.
Focus on problem-solving, visualisation, mathematical modelling, communication, computational thinking, and data analytics.
Emphasis on real-life applications and experiential learning.
Continuous assessment through projects, activities, and investigations is integral; move beyond summative examinations only.
Prescribed Books: NCERT Textbook for Class IX, CBSE Guidelines for Mathematics Laboratory in Schools (Class IX), NCERT Laboratory Manual.

competency_groups.title: Understanding Numbers and Real Number System
competency_groups.description: Develops understanding of numbers, including the set of real numbers and its properties.
competencies: Understand the concept of a rational number.
Represent rational numbers on the number line.
Understand the properties of rational numbers.
Explain the concept of density of rational numbers.
Compute decimal representation of rational numbers.
Understand the concept of irrational numbers.
Prove the irrationality.
Construct the square root spiral.

competency_groups.title: Algebraic Reasoning and Modelling
competency_groups.description: States and proves remainder theorem, factor theorem, division algorithm; models real-life situations in the form of equations.
competencies: Understand the meaning of an algebraic expression.
Define a polynomial.
Identify the degree, terms and coefficients of terms in a polynomial.
Model linear growth and decay using linear polynomials.
Explain and identify patterns in linear relationships.
Identify the slope and y-intercept of a linear equation in two variables.
Graph a linear equation in two variables.

competency_groups.title: Sequences and Computational Thinking
competency_groups.description: Models daily-life phenomena using representations; uses computational thinking for pattern recognition.
competencies: Understand the concept of a sequence of numbers.
Identify the pattern in a sequence and predict the next few terms.
Determine the recursive and explicit rules for different sequences.
Obtain the terms of sequence given its recursive and explicit rule.
Identify Arithmetic Progressions (AP).
Determine the nth term of an AP.
Visualise an AP graphically.
Identify Geometric Progressions (GP).
Determine the nth term of a GP.
Visualise a GP graphically.
Analyse attributes of fractals using GP.

competency_groups.title: Visualisation and Decomposition of Identities
competency_groups.description: Uses computational thinking strategies such as decomposition to visualise identities.
competencies: Visualise algebraic identities using geometric models.
Determine the factors of algebraic expressions using identities.
Interpret factors of quadratic expressions through geometric models.
Find simplified versions of rational expressions.

competency_groups.title: Linear Equations and Graphical Modelling
competency_groups.description: Models daily-life phenomena using representations; solves contextualised problems.
competencies: Understand the concept of a linear equation in two variables.
Graph a pair of linear equations.
Solve a pair of linear equations graphically.
Solve a pair of linear equations through substitution and elimination methods.
Determine the nature of solutions of a pair of linear equations.

competency_groups.title: Coordinate Geometry and Spatial Reasoning
competency_groups.description: Specifies locations using coordinates; applies computational thinking to model situations.
competencies: Specify locations and the position of one point relative to another point using coordinates.
Represent a floor plan on a grid using coordinates.
Compute the distance between two points using coordinates.
Determine whether three points lie in a straight line using coordinates.
Compute the position of the midpoint of a line segment using coordinates.

competency_groups.title: Axiomatic Structure and Constructions
competency_groups.description: Proves mathematical statements using axioms; carries out geometric constructions.
competencies: Describe how geometry grew from practical needs of ancient civilisations.
Understand the role of definitions, axioms, and postulates.
State the 5 postulates of Euclidean geometry.
Define parallelism of straight lines.
Justify simple constructions using the axioms.

competency_groups.title: Lines and Angles: Logical Reasoning
competency_groups.description: Follows proof by contradiction; applies computational thinking to analyse geometric ideas.
competencies: Explain the notion of an angle and a ray.
Classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, or reflex.
State and apply linear pair theorem and its converse.
Prove that vertically opposite angles are equal.
Identify corresponding, alternate, and interior angles.

competency_groups.title: Triangle Congruence Theorems
competency_groups.description: Proves theorems using Euclid's axioms; explains propositions and their converses.
competencies: Explain that a triangle is rigid.
Describe what it means for two triangles to be congruent.
Use SAS, SSS, ASA, RHS, AAS congruence conditions.
Prove basic properties of isosceles triangles.
Identify the converse of a given proposition.

competency_groups.title: Quadrilaterals and Symmetry
competency_groups.description: Proves theorems related to parallelograms; understands central symmetry.
competencies: Frame a precise definition of a 4-gon.
Prove various characterisations of a parallelogram.
Prove the midpoint theorem and its converse.
Understand reflection and rotation symmetries of 4-gons.

competency_groups.title: Circle Geometry and Properties
competency_groups.description: Proves theorems about circles; uses computational thinking to verify properties.
competencies: State the definition of a circle.
Explain meanings of chord, diameter, radius, arc, segment, sector.
Construct circumcircle and circumcentre of a triangle.
Prove relationships between chords, angles at centre, and cyclic quadrilaterals.

competency_groups.title: Mensuration: Area and Perimeter
competency_groups.description: Derives formulae for areas; uses computational thinking to break down shapes.
competencies: Define perimeter as the length around a shape.
Compute circumference of a circle and arc length.
Apply Heron's formula to compute area from sides.
Explain Brahmagupta's formula for cyclic quadrilaterals.

competency_groups.title: Surface Area and Volume Calculations
competency_groups.description: Derives formulae for surface areas and volumes; uses computational thinking to compare shapes.
competencies: Compute the surface area and volume of cuboids, cubes, cylinders, cones, pyramids, spheres.

competency_groups.title: Statistical Analysis and Data Interpretation
competency_groups.description: Applies measures of central tendency; uses computational thinking to analyse real-life data.
competencies: Collect, organise, visualise and interpret data.
Compute weighted average, mean, median, mode.

competency_groups.title: Probability and Randomness Modelling
competency_groups.description: Applies concepts from probability; uses computational thinking to model random experiments.
competencies: Understand the concept of randomness.
Describe likelihood using probability scale.
Estimate empirical probability by analysing data.
Define and compute theoretical probability.

question_paper_design.description: Exhibit memory of previously learned material by recalling facts, terms, basic concepts. Demonstrate understanding of facts and ideas by organizing, comparing, translating, interpreting.
Solve problems to new situations by applying acquired knowledge, facts, techniques and rules in a different way.
Examine and break information into parts by identifying motives or causes. Make inferences and find evidence to support generalizations.
Compile information together in a different way by combining elements in a new pattern or proposing alternative solutions.

competency_weightage: Competencies are integrated across all units with emphasis on: Problem-solving, Visualisation, Mathematical Modelling, Communication, Computational Thinking, and Data Analytics.
Deep conceptual understanding prioritized over rote learning.
internal_assessment: - Pen Paper Test and Multiple Assessment: 10 Marks (Split as 5+5)

practical_assessment.title: Lab Practical
practical_assessment.description: Lab activities to be done from the prescribed books.
prescribed_books: Guidelines for Mathematics Laboratory in Schools, Class IX (CBSE)

project_assessment.title: Portfolio Assessment
project_assessment.description: Continuous and holistic assessment through projects, activities, and investigations forms an integral part of the learning process.

outcomes: Understand the concept of a rational number.
Represent rational numbers on the number line.
Understand the properties of rational numbers.
Explain the concept of density of rational numbers.
Compute decimal representation of rational numbers.

learning_outcomes.topic: Irrational Numbers and Square Root Spiral
outcomes: Understand the concept of irrational numbers.
Prove the irrationality (e.g., √2, √3).
Construct the square root spiral.
Apply computational thinking to represent rational and irrational numbers through algorithms and visual models.
Generate decimal expansions systematically.
Understand the meaning of an algebraic expression.
Define a polynomial.
Identify the degree, terms and coefficients of terms in a polynomial.
Model linear growth and decay using linear polynomials.
Explain and identify patterns in linear relationships.
Identify the slope and y-intercept of a linear equation in two variables.
Graph a linear equation in two variables.
Use computational thinking to identify patterns, construct linear expressions, and systematically represent and analyse linear relationships using equations and graphs.
Understand the concept of a sequence of numbers.
Identify the pattern in a sequence and predict the next few terms.
Determine the recursive and explicit rules for different sequences.
Obtain the terms of sequence given its recursive and explicit rule.
Identify Arithmetic Progressions (AP).
Determine the nth term of an AP.
Visualise an AP graphically.
Identify Geometric Progressions (GP).
Determine the nth term of a GP.
Visualise a GP graphically.
Analyse attributes of fractals using GP.
Solve the Tower of Hanoi puzzle.
Use computational thinking to identify patterns, write step-by-step rules, and model patterns in sequences and progressions.
Visualise algebraic identities using geometric models.
Determine the factors of algebraic expressions using identities.
Interpret factors of quadratic expressions through geometric models.
Find simplified versions of rational expressions.
Use computational thinking strategies, such as decomposition and step-by-step procedures to visualise algebraic identities, factor expressions, and simplify rational expressions.
Understand the concept of a linear equation in two variables.
Graph a pair of linear equations.
Solve a pair of linear equations graphically.
Solve a pair of linear equations through the methods of substitution and elimination.
Determine the nature of solutions of a pair of linear equations.
Model and solve contextualised problems using a pair of linear equations and draw conclusions.
Model daily-life phenomena using representations, such as graphs, tables, and equations.
Use computational thinking to systematically represent, solve, and interpret pairs of linear equations through graphs, tables, and step-by-step procedures.
Specify locations and the position of one point relative to another point using coordinates.
Represent a floor plan on a grid using coordinates.
Compute the distance between two points using coordinates.
Determine whether three points lie in a straight line using coordinates.
Compute the position of the midpoint of a line segment using coordinates.
Check whether a triangle is right-angled using coordinates.
Apply computational thinking to model situations on the coordinate plane and verify geometric properties through systematic reasoning.
Describe how geometry grew from the practical needs ancient civilisations.
Describe contributions of India, Egypt and Greece to the development of geometric ideas.
Understand the role of definitions, axioms, and postulates.
Explain that there are elements of plane geometry (point, line, surface) for which we have an intuitive sense.
State the 5 postulates of Euclidean geometry.
Define parallelism of straight lines.
Explain the construction of a square as given in the Sulbasutras.
Justify simple constructions using the axioms.
Explain the notion of an angle.
Explain the notion of a ray.
Classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, or reflex.
State that a straight angle equals two right angles (180°) while a right angle measures 90°.
Define parallelism.
State and apply the linear pair theorem and its converse.
Follow proof by contradiction in geometry.
Prove that vertically opposite angles are equal.
Identify corresponding, alternate, and interior angles.
Explain transitivity of parallelism.

Apply computational thinking to analyse geometric ideas by breaking constructions into ordered steps, using axioms and postulates as rules, and justifying geometric results through logical step-by-step reasoning.
Explain that a triangle is rigid, unlike a quadrilateral.
Identify uses of triangle rigidity.
Describe what it means for two triangles to be congruent.
Identify correspondence between the vertices, sides, and angles of two congruent triangles.
Use the SAS congruence axiom.
Use the SSS congruence condition.
Use the RHS congruence condition.
Use the AAS congruence condition.
Prove the basic properties of isosceles triangles.
Explain the notion of a proposition.
Explain the notion of converse of a proposition.
Identify the converse of a given proposition.

learning_outcomes.topic: Validity of SSA and Diagram Accuracy
outcomes: Explain that not all converses are true; use counter examples to show that some converses are false.
Explain why SSA is not, in general, a valid congruence condition.
Identify the situations where SSA is a valid congruence condition.
Justify the role of diagram accuracy.
Frame a precise definition of a 4-gon.
Prove various characterisations of a parallelogram.
Prove the midpoint theorem.
Prove a converse of the midpoint theorem.
Prove that the medians of a triangle are concurrent and each median is divided in the ratio 2:1 at the point of concurrence.
Prove that the 4-gon formed by joining the midpoints of a given 4-gon is a parallelogram.
Understand reflection and rotation symmetries of 4-gons.
Understand how any 4-gon can tile a plane.
Practice forming logical converses of statements.
Engage in drawing, measurement and paper manipulation activities to discover geometric patterns involving triangles and 4-gons.
State the definition of a circle.
Explain the meanings of the terms 'chord', 'diameter', 'radius', 'arc', 'segment', and 'sector'.
Explain why there exists a unique circle through three non-collinear points.
Construct the circumcircle and circumcentre of a triangle.
Describe the location of the circumcentre for acute, obtuse, and right-angled triangles.
Explain what 'angle subtended by an arc at the centre' means.
Explain why 'equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre'.
Explain why 'the line from the centre of a circle to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord'.
Explain why 'a perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects the chord'.
State the relationship between length of a chord and its distance from the centre.
Explain why 'equal chords are equidistant from the centre (and conversely)'.
Explain why 'among unequal chords, the longer chord is closer to the centre'.
Explain why 'the diameter is the longest chord'.
Explain why 'the angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by the arc at any point on the remaining part of the circle'.
Explain why 'angles in the same segment of a circle are equal'.
Determine when four given points are concyclic.
Explain why 'a quadrilateral with supplementary opposite angles is cyclic, and conversely'.
Explain how circular wheels have influenced transport, farming, building, and technology.
Identify cultural motifs involving circles (e.g., Dharmachakra, Ashoka Chakra).
Use computational thinking to break down circle-related problems, apply geometric rules step-by-step, and verify properties of figures through systematic reasoning.
Define perimeter as the length around the boundary of any shape.
Explain that the circumference-to-diameter ratio is constant for all circles.
Compute the circumference of a circle and the length of an arc.
Apply ideas of circle perimeter and arc-length to real-world contexts.
Explain why a median of a triangle divides it into two triangles of equal area.
Use Heron's formula to compute the area of a triangle from its sides.
List historical approximations to π (from Archimedes, Aryabhata, Zu Chongzhi).

learning_outcomes.topic: Squaring a Rectangle and Ancient Civilisations
outcomes: Explain the classical problem of 'squaring' a given shape.
Explain how ancient civilisations approximated the area of a circle.
Compute the area of a circle using the formula.
Explain and use the formula for area of a sector of a circle.
Solve problems on areas of sectors and segments of circles.
State Brahmagupta's formula for the area of a cyclic quadrilateral in terms of its sides.
Explain why Heron's formula is a 'special case' of Brahmagupta's formula.
Explain the notion of 'special case' and 'generalisation' in mathematics.

Use computational thinking to break down shapes, apply step-by-step methods to calculate perimeter and area, recognise patterns across formulae, and understand generalisation and special cases in geometry.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
outcomes: Recognise cuboids and cubes in real-life situations.
Compute the surface area and volume of a cuboid.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
outcomes: Explain how a cube is a 'special case' of a cuboid.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
outcomes: Describe a right circular cylinder using its radius and height.
Compute the surface area and volume of a cylinder.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
outcomes: Recognise cones in daily life.
Compute the surface area and volume of a cone.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
outcomes: Recognise a pyramid.
Compute the surface area and volume of a pyramid.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
outcomes: Recognise spheres in real-life situations.
Compute the surface area and volume of a sphere.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)
outcomes: Use computational thinking to systematically calculate, and compare surface areas and volumes of 3-D shapes by varying dimensions and analysing patterns.
Collect, organise, visualise and interpret data to answer a statistical investigative question.
Compute and apply weighted average in different settings.
Read and interpret stacked bar graphs and 100% stacked bar graphs.
Apply computational thinking strategies to analyse real-life data, create appropriate graphical representations, and interpret mean, median and mode for decision-making.
Understand the concept of randomness.
Describe the likelihood of an event using the probability scale.
Estimate the empirical probability of the occurrence of an event by analysing statistical data.
Define theoretical probability of an event.
Apply the definition of theoretical probability to compute the probability of an event.
Compute probability of events with the help of tree diagrams and tables.
Use computational thinking strategies, such as pattern recognition and simulation, to model random experiments and estimate probabilities.

activities.title: Square Root Spiral Construction
activities.details: Construct the square root spiral. Apply computational thinking to represent rational and irrational numbers through algorithms and visual models.

activities.title: Tower of Hanoi Puzzle
activities.details: Solve the Tower of Hanoi puzzle. Use computational thinking to identify patterns, write step-by-step rules.

activities.title: Geometric Models for Identities
activities.details: Visualise algebraic identities using geometric models. Visualising factorisation of quadratic expressions through algebra tiles.

activities.title: Floor Plan Representation
activities.details: Represent a floor plan on a grid using coordinates. Compute the distance between two points.

activities.title: Euclid's Definitions and Constructions
activities.details: Constructing a square with a given side as described in the Baudhayana's Sulbasutras. Justify simple constructions using axioms.

activities.title: Paper Manipulation Activities
activities.details: Engage in drawing, measurement and paper manipulation activities to discover geometric patterns involving triangles and 4-gons.

activities.title: Cultural Motifs Analysis
activities.details: Identify cultural motifs involving circles, for example, the Dharmachakra, Ashoka Chakra, Sudarshan Chakra.

activities.title: Squaring a Rectangle Proof
activities.details: Explain the classical problem of 'squaring' a given shape. Explain how ancient civilisations approximated the area of a circle.

activities.title: Data Analysis Projects
activities.details: Collect, organise, visualise and interpret data to answer a statistical investigative question. Apply computational thinking strategies to analyse real-life data.

activities.title: Random Experiment Simulation
activities.details: Use computational thinking strategies, such as pattern recognition and simulation, to model random experiments and estimate probabilities.

projects.title: Portfolio Assessment
projects.details: Internal Assessment: Portfolio (05 Marks). Continuous and holistic assessment through projects, activities, and investigations forms an integral part of the learning process.

projects.title: Mathematical Modelling Projects
projects.details: Models daily-life phenomena and uses representations (graphs, tables, equations) to draw conclusions. Models real-life situations in the form of equations.

practicals.title: Lab Practical (Mathematics Laboratory)
practicals.details: Lab activities to be done from the prescribed books. Prescribed Books: Mathematics - Textbook for class IX, Guidelines for Mathematics Laboratory in Schools, class IX, Laboratory Manual - Mathematics, secondary stage.

lesson_planning_intelligence.concept_density: High (Abstract concepts of irrationality and density)
lesson_planning_intelligence.practical_requirement: Square Root Spiral Construction activity required.

lesson_planning_intelligence.assessment_requirement: Summative assessment on polynomial basics and linear relationships.
Summative assessment on AP/GP and fractals.
Summative assessment on factorization and simplification.
Summative assessment on solving equations and modelling.
Summative assessment on distance and midpoint formulas.
Summative assessment on postulates and constructions.
Summative assessment on angle properties and proofs.
Summative assessment on chord properties and angles.
Summative assessment on area and perimeter calculations.
Summative assessment on Heron's and Brahmagupta's formulas.

lesson_planning_intelligence.chapter_name: Surface Area and Volume (Cube, Cuboid, Cylinder, Cone, Pyramid, Sphere)

lesson_planning_intelligence.assessment_requirement: Summative assessment on data visualization and analysis.
Summative assessment on probability scales and calculations.
